content: Introduction to ELIZA   The ELIZA chatbot, created by Joseph Weizenbaum in the 1960s, is a landmark in the development of artificial intelligence (AI) and human-computer interaction. This chatbot was designed to explore the psychological factors that lead humans to misinterpret its capabilities, and its interactions with users led to the concept of the “ELIZA effect.” The ELIZA effect, as defined by sociologist Sherry Turkle, refers to the tendency to treat responsive computer programs as more intelligent than they really are. Similarly, cognitive and computer scientist Douglas Hofstadter describes the ELIZA effect as the susceptibility of people to read far more understanding than is warranted into strings of symbols.
description: Discover ELIZA, the pioneering chatbot that influenced AI development and human-computer interaction

Introduction to the Incident   The Iranian government has been reported to have abused vulnerabilities in the global telecoms infrastructure to locate U.S. military personnel in the build-up to the Iran War and in the early days of the conflict. This incident highlights the risks associated with the exploitation of telecoms infrastructure vulnerabilities, which can have severe consequences for national security.
The Exploited Vulnerabilities   The Iranian government exploited the Signaling System 7 (SS7) vulnerability, a well-known issue in the global telecoms infrastructure. SS7 is a set of protocols used in 2G and 3G networks, and its exploitation can allow attackers to track cellphones abroad. In addition to SS7, the Iranian government also abused advertising technology used to serve tailored ads to cellphone users. This abuse of advertising technology allowed the Iranian government to gather information about U.S. military personnel, including their locations.

Background on the Suspension   The t.me domain was suspended on the same day that the U.S. Treasury imposed sanctions on a VPN provider called First VPN. The Treasury’s sanctions listing for First VPN contained a link to the full web address of the VPN provider’s public group on Telegram using the shortened t.me domain. content: Introduction to UEFI Infections   The clock is ticking for Windows and Linux users to update their cryptographic keys that protect their systems against firmware-based UEFI infections. Beginning June 24, three certificates that cryptographically verify each piece of firmware and software that loads during system boot will expire. These Microsoft-signed certificates are the linchpins of Secure Boot, a Microsoft-designed chain of trust. Secure Boot checks the digital signatures of all firmware that loads during system startup to ensure it originates from a trusted provider, such as the manufacturer of the motherboard the system runs on.
